misconceptionSummary: Students confuse vertically opposite angles with adjacent angles and incorrectly add them to 180° instead of recognising they are equal.

lines.text: Two lines cross. One angle is 50°.
Subtracted from 180° — but opposite angles are equal

steps.narration: The student subtracted from one hundred and eighty and got one hundred and thirty. But one hundred and thirty is the adjacent angle — the one next to fifty degrees on the straight line. The angle directly opposite fifty is also fifty, because vertically opposite angles are equal.

visuals.ruleText: Opposite = across the crossing point = equal.  Adjacent = next door = supplementary (180°).
visuals.subText: The angle that is across from 50° is 50°, not 130°

steps.narration: At a crossing, every angle has two neighbours and one opposite. The two neighbours are supplementary — they add to one hundred and eighty. The opposite is equal. The distinction is direction: next to or across from.

With one angle of fifty: the opposite angle is fifty. Both adjacent angles are one hundred and thirty. The four angles — fifty, one hundred and thirty, fifty, one hundred and thirty — total three hundred and sixty as expected.

visuals.label: Opposite → equal (50°).  Adjacent → supplementary (130°).  Not the same.

steps.narration: Fifty degrees opposite, one hundred and thirty adjacent. Equal opposite, supplementary adjacent. The confusion arises from misidentifying which angle is which — always check whether the angle is across from or next to the known angle.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: opposite angle = 180° − 50° = 130°. That is the ADJACENT angle, not the opposite one. Vertically opposite angles are equal: opposite angle = 50°. Adjacent angles sum to 180°.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Opposite means across the crossing — equal. Adjacent means next door — supplementary (180°). The angle that is directly across from 50° is also 50°. The 130° angles are the ones beside it.
